gold_final_answer: n-1 if n is even; n if n is odd
topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Graph Theory > Matchings, Marriage Lemma, Tutte's theorem
Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
prompt:
There are $n$ football teams in *Tumbólia*.
A championship is to be organized in which each team plays against every other exactly once. Every match takes place on Sundays and one team must not play more than once in the same day.
Find the least integer $m$ for which it's possible to set up a championship lasting $m$ Sundays.
response_text:
Each Sunday can host at most \(\lfloor n/2\rfloor\) matches, since a team plays at most once that day.

There are \(\binom{n}{2}=\frac{n(n-1)}2\) matches total, so we need at least
\[
m \ge \frac{\binom{n}{2}}{\lfloor n/2\rfloor}.
\]

Now consider the two cases:

- **If \(n\) is even**, say \(n=2k\), then each Sunday can have at most \(k\) matches, and in fact a full round-robin schedule can be arranged in exactly \(n-1=2k-1\) Sundays.
- **If \(n\) is odd**, say \(n=2k+1\), then each Sunday can have at most \(k\) matches, and a round-robin schedule can be arranged in exactly \(n=2k+1\) Sundays.

These are standard and optimal.

So the least possible number of Sundays is
\[
m=
\begin{cases}
n-1, & \text{if } n \text{ is even},\\[4pt]
n, & \text{if } n \text{ is odd}.
\end{cases}
\]

Final answer: \(\displaystyle m=\begin{cases}n-1,& n\text{ even}\\ n,& n\text{ odd}\end{cases}\)
